revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Meigetsuki]][[es:Meigetsuki]][[fa:میگتسوکی]][[fr:Meigetsuki]][[ja:明月記]][[zh:明月記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Meigetsuki]][[es:Meigetsuki]][[fa:میگتسوکی]][[fr:Meigetsuki]][[ja:明月記]][[zh:明月記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Meigetsuki]][[es:Meigetsuki]][[fa:میگتسوکی]][[fr:Meigetsuki]][[ja:明月記]][[zh:明月記]]
[[File:Meigetsuki (Kitamura Museum).jpg|thumb|300px|明月記（北村美術館蔵）]] 『'''Meigetsuki'''』（明月記） is a {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}} diary written by {{ill|Fujiwara no Teika|en|Fujiwara no Teika|WD=Q378425|lt=Fujiwara no Teika}} (藤原定家), a nobleman and poet. The diary spans a meticulous 56-year record from {{ill|1180|en|1180|WD=Q19702|lt=1180}} (Jishō 4) to {{ill|1235|en|1235|WD=Q5425|lt=1235}} (Katei 1). It is also known by other names, such as '''Shōkōki''' (照光記) and '''Teikakyōki''' (定家卿記).
In 2019, it was designated a [[Japan Astronomical Heritage]].
== Overview ==
The diary of {{ill|Fujiwara no Teika|en|Fujiwara no Teika|WD=Q378425|lt=Fujiwara no Teika}}, who was highly regarded in [[:en:waka poetry|waka poetry]] (e.g., {{ill|Ogura Hyakunin Isshu|ja|小倉百人一首|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ogura Hyakunin Isshu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 百人一首|1=Hyakunin Isshu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1145042}}) and {{ill|Calligraphy|en|Calligraphy|WD=Q12681|lt=Calligraphy}}, became significant in later generations. The name "Meigetsuki" was given posthumously and not by Teika himself, who referred to it as "Guki" (愚記). After his death, Teika's descendants referred to it as "Chūnagon Nyūdō-den Nikki," but it was more commonly called "Teikakyōki."
By the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period}}, the name "Meigetsuki" had become standard. According to the {{ill|Hirohashike Kiroku|ja|広橋家記録|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirohashike Kiroku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a theory by {{ill|Nijō Yoshimoto|en|Nijō Yoshimoto|WD=Q541021|lt=Nijō Yoshimoto}} suggests that "Meigetsuki" refers to a diary created under divine guidance during a pilgrimage to {{ill|Sumiyoshi Myōjin|ja|住吉明神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Myōjin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1114013}}, though this remains unproven. However, the diary's role as a record of court practices and {{ill|family knowledge|ja|家学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/family knowledge|qq=}} likely led to its recognition as Teika's authoritative work, a perception embraced by his descendants and others.
Most of Teika's original manuscripts are preserved in the {{ill|Reizei-ke Shiguretei Bunko|ja|冷泉家時雨亭文庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reizei-ke Shiguretei Bunko|1=Reizei Family Shiguretei Library|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11395344}} and have been designated as {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure (Japan)}}. Parts of the original manuscripts were dispersed early as calligraphic treasures, including scrolls and hanging scrolls, and are now held in various collections such as the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|WD=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum}}, {{ill|Kyoto National Museum|en|Kyoto National Museum|WD=Q147286|lt=Kyoto National Museum}}, and {{ill|Tenri Central Library|en|Tenri Central Library|WD=Q872328|lt=Tenri University Library}}.
== Background ==
Teika's family, not traditionally associated with the "Houses of Diaries" (日記の家), was not politically privileged. Consequently, Teika likely wrote "Meigetsuki" to record his experiences and acquired knowledge, aiming to elevate himself and his descendants as a "House of Diaries."
However, Teika's renown in poetry and calligraphy ultimately overshadowed "Meigetsuki" as a bureaucratic reference. His son, {{ill|Fujiwara no Tameie|en|Fujiwara no Tameie|WD=Q535938|lt=Fujiwara no Tameie}}, transferred the diary to his illegitimate son, {{ill|Reizei Tamesuke|ja|冷泉為相|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reizei Tamesuke|1=Reizei Tamesuke|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10895612}}, acknowledging its diminished importance in the {{ill|Mikosa lineage|ja|御子左家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikosa lineage|1=Mikohidari family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488909}} of poets.
== Content ==
The diary holds value both as a historical record and as a scientific chronicle, though its classical Chinese prose is often challenging. While traditionally considered a 56-year record, references in Tameie's inheritance documents suggest it may extend until Teika's death in {{ill|1241|en|1241|WD=Q5436|lt=1241}}.
Research by scholars such as {{ill|Yashiro Kuniharu|ja|八代国治|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yashiro Kuniharu|1=Kuniji Yashiro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11390510}} has pointed out similarities between "Meigetsuki" and the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|en|Azuma Kagami|WD=Q1042324|lt=Azuma Kagami}}, likely due to Teika's connections with Kamakura-era officials.
=== Astronomical Records ===
The diary includes records of celestial phenomena, reflecting the era's belief in the ominous significance of unusual events. Its description of the {{ill|supernova|en|supernova|WD=Q3937|lt=supernova}} {{ill|SN 1054|en|SN 1054|WD=Q32037|lt=SN 1054}}, which created the {{ill|Crab Nebula|en|Crab Nebula|WD=Q10934|lt=Crab Nebula}}, is particularly notable. Although this predates Teika's lifetime, he documented it from earlier records.
=== Guest Stars ===
The term "guest star" (客星) referred to unfamiliar stars, encompassing supernovae, novae, and comets. "Meigetsuki" records an instance on {{ill|1230|en|1230|WD=Q5412|lt=1230}}, describing a comet observed as a dim, prominent light.
== Manuscripts ==
=== National Treasures ===
* Meigetsuki (Reizei-ke Shiguretei Bunko), 58 volumes.
=== Important Cultural Properties ===
* Various records held by institutions such as {{ill|Tenri Central Library|en|Tenri Central Library|WD=Q872328|lt=Tenri University Library}} and {{ill|Kyoto National Museum|en|Kyoto National Museum|WD=Q147286|lt=Kyoto National Museum}}.
== Related Literature ==
* "Meigetsuki Kōgi" (訓読明月記), {{ill|Imai Shoin|ja|今井書店|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imai Shoin|1=Imai Shoten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11376631}}, 2002. * {{ill|Fujimoto Kōichi|ja|藤本孝一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujimoto Kōichi|1=Fujimoto Kōichi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q95162028}}, "The World of Meigetsuki."
== References ==
{{Reflist}}

The term "guest star" (客星) referred to unfamiliar stars, encompassing supernovae, novae, and comets. "Meigetsuki" records an instance on {{ill|1230|en|1230|WD=Q5412|lt=1230|1=1230}}, describing a comet observed as a dim, prominent light.
== Manuscripts ==
=== National Treasures ===
* Meigetsuki (Reizei-ke Shiguretei Bunko), 58 volumes.
=== Important Cultural Properties ===
* Various records held by institutions such as {{ill|Tenri Central Library|en|Tenri Central Library|WD=Q872328|lt=Tenri University Library|1=Tenri Central Library}} and {{ill|Kyoto National Museum|en|Kyoto National Museum|WD=Q147286|lt=Kyoto National Museum|1=Kyoto National Museum}}.
== Related Literature ==
* "Meigetsuki Kōgi" (訓読明月記), {{ill|Imai Shoin|ja|今井書店|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imai Shoin|1=Imai Shoten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11376631}}, 2002. * {{ill|Fujimoto Kōichi|ja|藤本孝一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujimoto Kōichi|1=Q95162028|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q95162028}}, "The World of Meigetsuki."

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Meigetsuki]][[es:Meigetsuki]][[fa:میگتسوکی]][[fr:Meigetsuki]][[ja:明月記]][[zh:明月記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Meigetsuki]][[es:Meigetsuki]][[fa:میگتسوکی]][[fr:Meigetsuki]][[ja:明月記]][[zh:明月記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|commons:Category:Meigetsuki]][[es:Meigetsuki]][[fa:میگتسوکی]][[fr:Meigetsuki]][[ja:明月記]][[zh:明月記]]
[[File:Meigetsuki (Kitamura Museum).jpg|thumb|300px|明月記（北村美術館蔵）]] 『'''Meigetsuki'''』（明月記） is a {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}} diary written by {{ill|Fujiwara no Teika|en|Fujiwara no Teika|WD=Q378425|lt=Fujiwara no Teika|1=Fujiwara no Teika}} (藤原定家), a nobleman and poet. The diary spans a meticulous 56-year record from {{ill|1180|en|1180|WD=Q19702|lt=1180|1=1180}} (Jishō 4) to {{ill|1235|en|1235|WD=Q5425|lt=1235|1=1235}} (Katei 1). It is also known by other names, such as '''Shōkōki''' (照光記) and '''Teikakyōki''' (定家卿記).
In 2019, it was designated a [[Japan Astronomical Heritage]].
== Overview ==
The diary of {{ill|Fujiwara no Teika|en|Fujiwara no Teika|WD=Q378425|lt=Fujiwara no Teika|1=Fujiwara no Teika}}, who was highly regarded in [[:en:waka poetry|waka poetry]] (e.g., {{ill|Ogura Hyakunin Isshu|ja|小倉百人一首|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ogura Hyakunin Isshu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 百人一首|1=Ogura Hyakunin Isshu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1145042}}) and {{ill|Calligraphy|en|Calligraphy|WD=Q12681|lt=Calligraphy|1=Calligraphy}}, became significant in later generations. The name "Meigetsuki" was given posthumously and not by Teika himself, who referred to it as "Guki" (愚記). After his death, Teika's descendants referred to it as "Chūnagon Nyūdō-den Nikki," but it was more commonly called "Teikakyōki."
By the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period|1=Nanboku-chō period}}, the name "Meigetsuki" had become standard. According to the {{ill|Hirohashike Kiroku|ja|広橋家記録|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirohashike Kiroku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a theory by {{ill|Nijō Yoshimoto|en|Nijō Yoshimoto|WD=Q541021|lt=Nijō Yoshimoto|1=Nijō Yoshimoto}} suggests that "Meigetsuki" refers to a diary created under divine guidance during a pilgrimage to {{ill|Sumiyoshi Myōjin|ja|住吉明神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Myōjin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1114013}}, though this remains unproven. However, the diary's role as a record of court practices and {{ill|family knowledge|ja|家学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/family knowledge|qq=}} likely led to its recognition as Teika's authoritative work, a perception embraced by his descendants and others.
Most of Teika's original manuscripts are preserved in the {{ill|Reizei-ke Shiguretei Bunko|ja|冷泉家時雨亭文庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reizei-ke Shiguretei Bunko|1=Reizei Family Shiguretei Library|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11395344}} and have been designated as {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure (Japan)|1=National Treasure (Japan)}}. Parts of the original manuscripts were dispersed early as calligraphic treasures, including scrolls and hanging scrolls, and are now held in various collections such as the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|WD=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum|1=Tokyo National Museum}}, {{ill|Kyoto National Museum|en|Kyoto National Museum|WD=Q147286|lt=Kyoto National Museum|1=Kyoto National Museum}}, and {{ill|Tenri Central Library|en|Tenri Central Library|WD=Q872328|lt=Tenri University Library|1=Tenri Central Library}}.
== Background ==
Teika's family, not traditionally associated with the "Houses of Diaries" (日記の家), was not politically privileged. Consequently, Teika likely wrote "Meigetsuki" to record his experiences and acquired knowledge, aiming to elevate himself and his descendants as a "House of Diaries."
However, Teika's renown in poetry and calligraphy ultimately overshadowed "Meigetsuki" as a bureaucratic reference. His son, {{ill|Fujiwara no Tameie|en|Fujiwara no Tameie|WD=Q535938|lt=Fujiwara no Tameie|1=Fujiwara no Tameie}}, transferred the diary to his illegitimate son, {{ill|Reizei Tamesuke|ja|冷泉為相|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reizei Tamesuke|1=Reizei Tamesuke|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10895612}}, acknowledging its diminished importance in the {{ill|Mikosa lineage|ja|御子左家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikosa lineage|1=Mikohidari family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488909}} of poets.
== Content ==
The diary holds value both as a historical record and as a scientific chronicle, though its classical Chinese prose is often challenging. While traditionally considered a 56-year record, references in Tameie's inheritance documents suggest it may extend until Teika's death in {{ill|1241|en|1241|WD=Q5436|lt=1241|1=1241}}.
Research by scholars such as {{ill|Yashiro Kuniharu|ja|八代国治|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yashiro Kuniharu|1=Kuniji Yashiro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11390510}} has pointed out similarities between "Meigetsuki" and the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|en|Azuma Kagami|WD=Q1042324|lt=Azuma Kagami|1=Azuma Kagami}}, likely due to Teika's connections with Kamakura-era officials.
=== Astronomical Records ===
The diary includes records of celestial phenomena, reflecting the era's belief in the ominous significance of unusual events. Its description of the {{ill|supernova|en|supernova|WD=Q3937|lt=supernova|1=Supernova}} {{ill|SN 1054|en|SN 1054|WD=Q32037|lt=SN 1054|1=SN 1054}}, which created the {{ill|Crab Nebula|en|Crab Nebula|WD=Q10934|lt=Crab Nebula|1=Crab Nebula}}, is particularly notable. Although this predates Teika's lifetime, he documented it from earlier records.
=== Guest Stars ===
The term "guest star" (客星) referred to unfamiliar stars, encompassing supernovae, novae, and comets. "Meigetsuki" records an instance on {{ill|1230|en|1230|WD=Q5412|lt=1230|1=1230}}, describing a comet observed as a dim, prominent light.
== Manuscripts ==
=== National Treasures ===
* Meigetsuki (Reizei-ke Shiguretei Bunko), 58 volumes.
=== Important Cultural Properties ===
* Various records held by institutions such as {{ill|Tenri Central Library|en|Tenri Central Library|WD=Q872328|lt=Tenri University Library|1=Tenri Central Library}} and {{ill|Kyoto National Museum|en|Kyoto National Museum|WD=Q147286|lt=Kyoto National Museum|1=Kyoto National Museum}}.
== Related Literature ==
* "Meigetsuki Kōgi" (訓読明月記), {{ill|Imai Shoin|ja|今井書店|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imai Shoin|1=Imai Shoten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11376631}}, 2002. * {{ill|Fujimoto Kōichi|ja|藤本孝一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujimoto Kōichi|1=Q95162028|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q95162028}}, "The World of Meigetsuki."

While traditionally considered a 56-year record, references in Tameie's inheritance documents suggest it may extend until Teika's death in {{ill|1241|en|1241|WD=Q5436|lt=1241|1=1241}}.
Research by scholars such as {{ill|Yashiro Kuniharu|ja|八代国治|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yashiro Kuniharu|1=Kuniji Yashiro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11390510}} has pointed out similarities between "Meigetsuki" and the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|en|Azuma Kagami|WD=Q1042324|lt=Azuma Kagami|1=Azuma Kagami}}, likely due to Teika's connections with Kamakura-era officials.
=== Astronomical Records ===
The diary includes records of celestial phenomena, reflecting the era's belief in the ominous significance of unusual events. Its description of the {{ill|supernova|en|supernova|WD=Q3937|lt=supernova|1=Supernova}} {{ill|SN 1054|en|SN 1054|WD=Q32037|lt=SN 1054|1=SN 1054}}, which created the {{ill|Crab Nebula|en|Crab Nebula|WD=Q10934|lt=Crab Nebula|1=Crab Nebula}}, is particularly notable. Although this predates Teika's lifetime, he documented it from earlier records.
=== Guest Stars ===
The term "guest star" (客星) referred to unfamiliar stars, encompassing supernovae, novae, and comets. "Meigetsuki" records an instance on {{ill|1230|en|1230|WD=Q5412|lt=1230|1=1230}}, describing a comet observed as a dim, prominent light.
== Manuscripts ==
=== National Treasures ===
* Meigetsuki (Reizei-ke Shiguretei Bunko), 58 volumes.
=== Important Cultural Properties ===
* Various records held by institutions such as {{ill|Tenri Central Library|en|Tenri Central Library|WD=Q872328|lt=Tenri University Library|1=Tenri Central Library}} and {{ill|Kyoto National Museum|en|Kyoto National Museum|WD=Q147286|lt=Kyoto National Museum|1=Kyoto National Museum}}.
== Related Literature ==
* "Meigetsuki Kōgi" (訓読明月記), {{ill|Imai Shoin|ja|今井書店|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imai Shoin|1=Imai Shoten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11376631}}, 2002. * {{ill|Fujimoto Kōichi|ja|藤本孝一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujimoto Kōichi|1=Q95162028|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q95162028}}, "The World of Meigetsuki."

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Meigetsuki]][[es:Meigetsuki]][[fa:میگتسوکی]][[fr:Meigetsuki]][[ja:明月記]][[zh:明月記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Meigetsuki]][[es:Meigetsuki]][[fa:میگتسوکی]][[fr:Meigetsuki]][[ja:明月記]][[zh:明月記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[es:Meigetsuki]][[fa:میگتسوکی]][[fr:Meigetsuki]][[ja:明月記]][[zh:明月記]]
[[File:Meigetsuki (Kitamura Museum).jpg|thumb|300px|明月記（北村美術館蔵）]] 『'''Meigetsuki'''』（明月記） is a {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}} diary written by {{ill|Fujiwara no Teika|en|Fujiwara no Teika|qid=Q378425|lt=Fujiwara no Teika|1=Fujiwara no Teika}} (藤原定家), a nobleman and poet. The diary spans a meticulous 56-year record from {{ill|1180|en|1180|qid=Q19702|lt=1180|1=1180}} (Jishō 4) to {{ill|1235|en|1235|qid=Q5425|lt=1235|1=1235}} (Katei 1). It is also known by other names, such as '''Shōkōki''' (照光記) and '''Teikakyōki''' (定家卿記).
In 2019, it was designated a [[Japan Astronomical Heritage]].
== Overview ==
The diary of {{ill|Fujiwara no Teika|en|Fujiwara no Teika|qid=Q378425|lt=Fujiwara no Teika|1=Fujiwara no Teika}}, who was highly regarded in [[:en:waka poetry|waka poetry]] (e.g., {{ill|Ogura Hyakunin Isshu|ja|小倉百人一首|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ogura Hyakunin Isshu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 百人一首|1=Ogura Hyakunin Isshu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1145042}}) and {{ill|Calligraphy|en|Calligraphy|qid=Q12681|lt=Calligraphy|1=Calligraphy}}, became significant in later generations. The name "Meigetsuki" was given posthumously and not by Teika himself, who referred to it as "Guki" (愚記). After his death, Teika's descendants referred to it as "Chūnagon Nyūdō-den Nikki," but it was more commonly called "Teikakyōki."
By the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|qid=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period|1=Nanboku-chō period}}, the name "Meigetsuki" had become standard. According to the {{ill|Hirohashike Kiroku|ja|広橋家記録|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirohashike Kiroku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a theory by {{ill|Nijō Yoshimoto|en|Nijō Yoshimoto|qid=Q541021|lt=Nijō Yoshimoto|1=Nijō Yoshimoto}} suggests that "Meigetsuki" refers to a diary created under divine guidance during a pilgrimage to {{ill|Sumiyoshi Myōjin|ja|住吉明神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Myōjin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 住吉三神|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1114013}}, though this remains unproven. However, the diary's role as a record of court practices and {{ill|family knowledge|ja|家学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/family knowledge|qq=}} likely led to its recognition as Teika's authoritative work, a perception embraced by his descendants and others.
Most of Teika's original manuscripts are preserved in the {{ill|Reizei-ke Shiguretei Bunko|ja|冷泉家時雨亭文庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reizei-ke Shiguretei Bunko|1=Reizei Family Shiguretei Library|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11395344}} and have been designated as {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|qid=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure (Japan)|1=National Treasure (Japan)}}. Parts of the original manuscripts were dispersed early as calligraphic treasures, including scrolls and hanging scrolls, and are now held in various collections such as the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|qid=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum|1=Tokyo National Museum}}, {{ill|Kyoto National Museum|en|Kyoto National Museum|qid=Q147286|lt=Kyoto National Museum|1=Kyoto National Museum}}, and {{ill|Tenri Central Library|en|Tenri Central Library|qid=Q872328|lt=Tenri University Library|1=Tenri Central Library}}.
== Background ==
Teika's family, not traditionally associated with the "Houses of Diaries" (日記の家), was not politically privileged. Consequently, Teika likely wrote "Meigetsuki" to record his experiences and acquired knowledge, aiming to elevate himself and his descendants as a "House of Diaries."
However, Teika's renown in poetry and calligraphy ultimately overshadowed "Meigetsuki" as a bureaucratic reference. His son, {{ill|Fujiwara no Tameie|en|Fujiwara no Tameie|qid=Q535938|lt=Fujiwara no Tameie|1=Fujiwara no Tameie}}, transferred the diary to his illegitimate son, {{ill|Reizei Tamesuke|ja|冷泉為相|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reizei Tamesuke|1=Reizei Tamesuke|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10895612}}, acknowledging its diminished importance in the {{ill|Mikosa lineage|ja|御子左家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikosa lineage|1=Mikohidari family|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11488909}} of poets.
== Content ==
The diary holds value both as a historical record and as a scientific chronicle, though its classical Chinese prose is often challenging. While traditionally considered a 56-year record, references in Tameie's inheritance documents suggest it may extend until Teika's death in {{ill|1241|en|1241|qid=Q5436|lt=1241|1=1241}}.
Research by scholars such as {{ill|Yashiro Kuniharu|ja|八代国治|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yashiro Kuniharu|1=Kuniji Yashiro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11390510}} has pointed out similarities between "Meigetsuki" and the {{ill|Azuma Kagami|en|Azuma Kagami|qid=Q1042324|lt=Azuma Kagami|1=Azuma Kagami}}, likely due to Teika's connections with Kamakura-era officials.
=== Astronomical Records ===
The diary includes records of celestial phenomena, reflecting the era's belief in the ominous significance of unusual events. Its description of the {{ill|supernova|en|supernova|qid=Q3937|lt=supernova|1=Supernova}} {{ill|SN 1054|en|SN 1054|qid=Q32037|lt=SN 1054|1=SN 1054}}, which created the {{ill|Crab Nebula|en|Crab Nebula|qid=Q10934|lt=Crab Nebula|1=Crab Nebula}}, is particularly notable. Although this predates Teika's lifetime, he documented it from earlier records.
=== Guest Stars ===
The term "guest star" (客星) referred to unfamiliar stars, encompassing supernovae, novae, and comets. "Meigetsuki" records an instance on {{ill|1230|en|1230|qid=Q5412|lt=1230|1=1230}}, describing a comet observed as a dim, prominent light.
== Manuscripts ==

* Meigetsuki (Reizei-ke Shiguretei Bunko), 58 volumes.
=== Important Cultural Properties ===
* Various records held by institutions such as {{ill|Tenri Central Library|en|Tenri Central Library|qid=Q872328|lt=Tenri University Library|1=Tenri Central Library}} and {{ill|Kyoto National Museum|en|Kyoto National Museum|qid=Q147286|lt=Kyoto National Museum|1=Kyoto National Museum}}.
